danger_signals.visual_form: Rage meter (怒) filling and glowing at screen edge
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent or player entering heightened combat state with enhanced damage potential; immediate danger escalation

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual hit feedback and weapon collision sparks on-screen
danger_signals.meaning: Physical threat materialization; active combat engagement danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Opponent positioning with drawn/active weapon in close range
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent melee danger; weapon-based threat proximity

safety_signals.visual_form: Characters at maximum stage separation with weapons lowered
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary reprieve from immediate combat threat; safe positioning

safety_signals.visual_form: Character in idle/ready position without aggressive animation
Referee visible in background officiating match
safety_signals.meaning: Structural authority maintaining match integrity; organized combat containment

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Traditional Japanese clothing, mon (crests), feudal period aesthetics
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Characters like Hattori Hanzō and Yagyū Jūbei appear as both playable and opponent variants based on narrative branch
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: 18th-century Japanese setting (Sakoku period, 1788-1789); traditional architecture implied through stage design

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Non-Japanese aesthetic elements; fictional monsters and foreign-born characters; supernatural visual distinctiveness
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Visually differentiated from core Japanese warrior aesthetic through costume and design language
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Incorporated into 18th-century Japan setting via artistic license; otherworldly or exotic visual markers

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Title-specific narrative focal points; boss-level visual distinction from roster characters
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Boss encounter visual design distinguishes them from standard opponent roster
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Narrative centerpoints referenced in game titles (Amakusa's Revenge, Blades of Blood)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 18th-century Japanese setting with period-appropriate architecture, clothing, and weaponry; traditional aesthetic dominance
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters engage in duelist combat; honor-based warrior etiquette reflected in dialogue and interjections

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fictional monsters and otherworldly characters present alongside historical warriors; blending of realistic and fantastic aesthetics
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Supernatural combatants coexist with grounded warrior archetypes; anomalous narrative elements

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Referee present in background; formal stage arenas with spectator-implied environments
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Formal combat rules enforced; match progression follows tournament or duel structure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rage gauge visual increase as player receives damage; character health depletion indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Damaged characters move with altered animations; player behavior shifts to desperate or aggressive patterns as health decreases

environmental_storytelling.example: 18th-century Japan backdrop establishes political context (Sakoku/seclusion period); settings communicate isolationist world-state and feudal hierarchy through visual period markers
Inclusion of historical figures (Hattori Hanzō, Yagyū Jūbei) alongside fictional warriors visually communicates political factions and power structures within setting through character selection

Game titles highlighting specific antagonists (Amakusa, Zankurō) communicate narrative conflict centers and world-state threats; visual prominence of these characters in marketing and story frames political tension
Dialogue exclusively in Japanese with archaic formality and modern slang variants; linguistic markers communicate cultural authenticity and social hierarchies through speech register
Traditional Japanese instruments (shakuhachi, shamisen, koto, taiko) in soundtrack visually-adjacent storytelling reinforces cultural setting and historical period immersion

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Standard attack patterns; moderate damage output; cautious positioning
Enhanced damage multiplier; altered attack animations; aggressive positioning incentive; heightened threat output
Animation shifts toward desperate/rapid attack patterns; increased aggression to compensate for survival pressure
Visible in background; officiating presence communicates rule structure enforcement; neutral arbiter role
Visually distinct attack chains; non-human movement patterns; otherworldly animation tells differentiate from historical warrior behavioral baseline

notes: Samurai Shodown (1993 arcade, 1994 Genesis port) communicates world-state primarily through aesthetic authenticity (18th-century Japanese setting) and mechanical systems (Rage gauge). Faction distinction occurs through character design language rather than explicit visual UI markers—Japanese warriors vs. foreign/supernatural combatants are visually differentiated through costume and design philosophy. Danger signals are mechanical (Rage gauge, health depletion) rather than environmental. The series emphasizes cultural and linguistic authenticity as world-state communication (Japanese-only dialogue, traditional instruments, historical setting). Narrative threats (Amakusa, Zankurō) are communicated through character prominence and title emphasis rather than dynamic world-state visualization. Genesis port maintains core visual communication systems from arcade original.
sources_used: wikipedia - Samurai Shodown series overview (plot, characters, development, games table, musical/linguistic design elements)
